1.1: Superscription — baqqar (sheep breeder) is Amos' occupation; the same word describes his herd at 7:14 where he also claims to be a boqer (cattle herder). The two terms may indicate he was a rancher of mixed flocks. Tekoa lay in the Judean wilderness ~10 miles south of Jerusalem, in territory remote from the northern shrines he will condemn. The date formula ('in the days of Uzziah… and Jeroboam') spans two prosperous kingdoms; the earthquake (hara'ash) is archaeologically attested at Hazor ~760 BC and independently referenced at Zech 14:5. The book anchors itself historically with unusual precision.
1.2: The LORD roars — yish'ag YHWH: the lion-roar image opens the book and echoes at 3:4, 3:8. The roar comes from Tsiyon… Yerushalam, not from Bethel or Dan — Amos, from Judah, locates YHWH's legitimate dwelling in the south despite preaching in the north. Ve-avlu ne'ot haro'im (the shepherds' pastures wither in mourning) — avalu from 'aval (mourn, dry up); the word play: mourning and withering are the same verb. Carmel's drying summit (rosh hakarmel) signals a judgment so total it reaches even the most fertile coastland.
1.3: Three… and for four — the formula 'al-shloshat pish'ei X ve'al-arba'ah lo ashivennu is a numerical graded idiom (x, x+1) expressing maximal accumulation, not literal counting. Pasha' (crime, transgression) — the root denotes covenant-rebellion, breaking an established relationship; its use for foreign nations is significant since it implies they are accountable to a moral order even without Israel's explicit covenant. Lo ashivennu (I will not revoke it/turn it back) — the antecedent is judgment; the decree is irrevocable. Damascus is judged for doshan ba-charitsot habarzel et-hagi-ad (threshing Gilead with iron sledges) — agricultural implements as instruments of mass violence.
1.4: Fire on the house of Hazael — ve-shilachti esh (I will send fire) — the divine fire formula recurs in every oracle (vv7, 10, 12, 14; 2:2, 5). Azalah (it will consume/devour) — kal perfect; fire is the standard covenant-curse for defeated cities (Deut 13:16). Armunot Ben-hadad (fortresses of Ben-hadad) — Ben-hadad is a dynastic name/title for Aramean kings; the palace-fortresses (armon) are symbols of royal power and the objects of judgment in every oracle.
1.5: Break the gate-bar of Damascus — shavar berich Dammeseq: berich (gate-bar, crossbar) is the wooden or metal beam that secured the city gate; breaking it = military defeat and open access to the enemy. Toshav biqat-Aven (ruler of the Valley of Aven) — Aven (nothingness/wickedness) may be a polemic alteration of the place name 'On.' Ve-galah 'am Aram Qirah — exile to Kir; this was fulfilled (2 Kgs 16:9) and reverses Amos 9:7 which says Aram came from Kir.
1.6: Three crimes of Gaza — Gaza represents Philistia. The crime is galotam galut shelemah lehassigir le-Edom (they deported a whole/complete captive population to hand over to Edom) — galut shelemah (complete deportation; shelemah = wholeness; not leaving survivors) is a specific aggravation. The crime is slave-trading: not conquest for territorial expansion but selling human beings for profit. Edom appears as the buyer, anticipating the Edom oracle (1:11-12).
1.7: Fire on the wall of Gaza — the same fire formula; chomah Azzah (wall of Gaza) vs. the earlier bet Chaza'el (house of Hazael); in each oracle the fire-object varies: house, wall, fortresses. The judgment is calibrated to the symbol of each nation's power.
1.8: Cut off the ruler of Ashdod — yashav (ruler, inhabitant) used as a title. Four Philistine cities are named (Ashdod, Ashkelon, Ekron — Gath notably absent, perhaps already destroyed). Va-hashivoti yadi 'al-Eqron (I will turn my hand against Ekron) — hashiv yad (turn the hand) is a gesture of attack. Ve-avad she'erit pelishtim (the remnant of the Philistines will perish) — she'erit (remnant) ironically used: the saving remnant concept inverted; their remnant will be destroyed.
1.9: Three crimes of Tyre — same formula. Tyre's crime is higgiram galut shelemah le-Edom (they handed over a complete deportation to Edom) — same phrase as Gaza (1:6). The added aggravation: ve-lo zakeru brit achim (they did not remember the covenant of brotherhood). Brit achim is unique in the OT; it most likely refers to the Hiram-Solomon treaty (achim = brothers as treaty partners, 1 Kgs 9:13). The commercial slave-trade violated a standing covenant relationship.
1.10: Fire on the wall of Tyre — the fire formula repeated; ve-akhlah armnoteiha (it will consume her fortresses). Tyre's commercial fortresses (armonot) were famous in antiquity; their consumption by fire was fulfilled in 332 BC by Alexander.
1.11: Three crimes of Edom — Edom's crime is described in psychological depth unusual in the oracle series: three parallel phrases. Radof va-cherev achiv (he hunted down his brother with the sword) — radaf (hunt, pursue relentlessly); achiv (his brother) = Jacob/Israel; the Esau-Jacob sibling relationship underlies the accusation. Ve-shiches rachamav — the MKT renders 'stifled all compassion'; shacat (corrupt, destroy) applied to rachamim (compassions, womb-feelings). Ve-tipposh le'olam appam (his anger maintained without end) — tipposh (retained, kept permanently) for sustained rage.
1.12: Fire on Teman — Teman was a major city in Edom's north; Bozrah (modern Buseirah) in the south. Both fortress-cities targeted, covering the nation's extent.
1.13: Three crimes of the Ammonites — the crime is described with the most graphic specificity: be-vaq'am haroth ha-Gil'ad (because they tore open [ripped] pregnant women of Gilead) — baqa' (cleave, split open) used for the atrocity of Elisha's prediction (2 Kgs 8:12) and Hazael's actions. The purpose: lema'an harkhiv et-gevulam (in order to extend their territory) — territorial expansion as the motive for this atrocity against civilian non-combatants.
1.14: Fire on Rabbah — berir'at Rabbah (on the wall of Rabbah) — Ammon's capital. The judgment is amplified with the war scene: bithru'ah beyom milchamah (amid battle cries on the day of war) and bise'arah beyom suphah (a storm on the day of whirlwind) — the double-day intensification formula appears also in eschatological judgment texts.
1.15: Their king will go into exile — malcam — could be the king (proper noun) or Milcom, the Ammonite deity (Zeph 1:5; 1 Kgs 11:5). The ambiguity is textually present: the king and the national deity are both exiled together with the officials (sarav). Exile of the deity in ANE thought was the ultimate defeat — YHWH overcomes both human and divine powers of Ammon.

2.1: Three crimes of Moab — the crime is desecration of the dead: be-sarfo atsmot melek-Edom la-sid (because he burned the bones of Edom's king to lime). Sid (lime, whitewash) — the bones were burned to produce caustic lime, either for use in construction or as a final act of desecration. The victim is the king of Edom — the same Edom who appears as perpetrator in 1:6, 9, 11. The criminal irony: Moab committed this atrocity against Edom, yet YHWH holds Moab accountable. International justice operates above ethnic alliances.
2.2: Fire on Moab — ve-met Moav basha'on (Moab will fall/die in turmoil) — sha'on (tumult, uproar) complements the battle imagery. Beteri'ah bequol shofar (with shouts and the sound of the ram's horn) — the cultic-military horn (shofar) is both the alarm of battle and the instrument of judgment.
2.3: Cut off the ruler — shofet (ruler, judge) is Moab's title here vs. yashav for the Philistine cities; the term may reflect the type of governance. The princes (sarim) fall together with the leader — total governmental collapse.
2.4: Three crimes of Judah — the oracle turns from foreign nations to the covenant people. The crime is specifically framed in covenant terms: me'asam et-Torat YHWH (they rejected the instruction/Torah of the LORD) and chuqqav lo shamar (they did not keep his statutes). Toehu (their false gods) — to'im (wanderings/falsehoods) — the idols are named by what they cause: straying, wandering. The formulaic crime-accusation shifts from specific atrocities (foreign nations) to covenant-failure (Judah), escalating the moral framework.
2.5: Fire on Judah — Ve-shilachti esh biYehudah: Judah receives the same fire-formula as the pagan nations. Armunot Yerushalam (fortresses of Jerusalem) — the capital's defensive structures face the same fate as Damascus, Gaza, and Tyre. The symmetry is deliberate and shocking: covenant status does not exempt from covenant-curse.
2.6: Three crimes of Israel — the climactic oracle, twice as long as the others. The crime list begins with economic: makhram ba-keseph tsaddiq ve'evyon ba'avur na'alayim (they sell the innocent for silver and the needy for a pair of sandals). Tsaddiq (innocent, righteous person) — someone who should prevail in a legal dispute but is sold out; evyon (needy/poor one) — the technical term for the destitute class. Ba'avur na'alayim (for sandals) — trivially small debt used to justify enslaving a person (cf. Neh 5:1-5).
2.7: Trample the heads of the poor — ha-sha'afim 'al-afar erets be-rosh dallim: sha'af (trample/pant after) combined with the image of pushing the head of the poor into the earth-dust (afar). Ve-derekh anavim yattu (push the afflicted off the road) — natah derekh (bend/divert the path) as a technical metaphor for denying legal process. The second accusation: ve-ish u-aviv yelkhu el-hanna'arah (father and son go to the same young woman) — sexual exploitation either of cult prostitutes or of female slaves, both violating the prohibition of Lev 18:15. Lechalel et-shem qodshi (to profane my holy name) — the climax: social sin profanes the divine name.
2.8: Pledged garments — begadim chavolim: clothes taken as debt-collateral (chaval = pledge) that were forbidden to be kept overnight (Exod 22:25-26; Deut 24:12-13). Spreading them out etsel kol-mizbeach (beside every altar) is doubly condemned: the stolen pledge is used in cultic celebration. Yeshet anushim (wine from court fines) — wine purchased with extorted legal settlements, consumed in the very house of God. The sacred space is profaned by the proceeds of economic injustice.
2.9: Yet I destroyed the Amorite — YHWH shifts to first-person recitation of saving history, contrasting past acts of grace with present ingratitude. Emori (Amorite) = the pre-Israelite Canaanite population collectively. The hyperbolic description — ka'asher gavohah aruzim gobho (whose height was like the cedars) and chazaq hu ke-allonim (strong as the oaks) — is mythologizing rhetoric emphasizing the supernatural difficulty YHWH overcame. Va'ashamed peryov mima'al ve-shorasho mittachat (I cut off his fruit above and his roots below) — total uprooting: neither fruit (offspring) nor root (lineage) survived.
2.10: I brought you up from Egypt — the exodus formula introduces the covenant basis for heightened accountability. Va'ohel etchem ba-midbar arba'im shanah (I led you through the wilderness forty years) — the wilderness period as sustained divine provision. Lareshet et-erets ha-Emori (to possess the land of the Amorite) — the conquest as the fulfillment of the exodus; YHWH's acts are a unified sequence (exodus → wilderness → conquest) that Israel owes entirely to divine initiative.
2.11: I raised up prophets and Nazirites — YHWH's provision was not only material (land, food) but prophetic. Nazirites (nezurim) — those under a vow of consecration (Num 6): no wine, no cutting of hair, no contact with the dead. Raising up both prophets and Nazirites represents both word and life-witness. The rhetorical question — ha-lo-khen bene Yisra'el? (is this not so, O people of Israel?) — demands acknowledgment of the gracious provision.
2.12: But you gave the Nazirites wine — the two provisions are deliberately violated: ve-tashqu et-haNezurim yayin (gave the Nazirites wine) and ve-'al-haNevi'im tsivitem le'mor lo tinnave'u (ordered the prophets: do not prophesy). Both acts are institutional silencing of divine communication: corrupting the life-witness of the Nazirite and gagging the verbal witness of the prophet. The people actively suppressed the very means of grace YHWH provided.
2.13: I will crush you in your tracks — the punishment oracle uses agricultural imagery. Hinneh anochi me'iq tachetechem — meiq is hapax; most derive from 'uq (press, oppress) or 'iq (make to groan). The image: YHWH himself as the crushing weight. Ka'asher ta'iq ha'agalah ha-mele'ah lah (as a cart loaded with sheaves presses down) — the threshing cart's impossible load pressing into the ground; Israel has loaded YHWH's patience past its limit.
2.14: Flight will fail the swift — three paired failure-of-escape vignettes: swift (kal) finds no flight, strong (chazaq) finds no strength, warrior (gibbor) saves not his life. The irony is that military capacity — speed, strength, skill — is exactly what fails. Each verse introduces another category of military professional rendered helpless.
2.15: The bowman will not hold his ground — ve-tofa'-qeshet lo ya'amod: continuing the cascade of military failure. Tofa' qeshet (handler of the bow) — ranged weaponry that should allow escape also fails. Qal beregalav lo yimalet (the swift-footed will not escape) — speed again inverted; parash susim lo yemallet nafsho (horseman will not save his life) — even mounted cavalry cannot outrun divine judgment.
2.16: The most courageous warrior will flee naked — ve-amits libbo ba-giborim (the most courageous of heart among the warriors) — the superlative: the bravest of the brave. Arom yanus bayom hahu (will flee naked on that day) — arom (naked): stripped of armor, of dignity, of everything. Ba-yom hahu (on that day) — the Day of the LORD formula appears here for the first time in Amos, linking military defeat to the eschatological judgment theme developed in 5:18-20.

3.1: Hear this word — shim'u et-haddavar hazeh: the prophetic call to attention that opens each major section (3:1; 4:1; 5:1). Asher dibber YHWH 'aleykhem (which the LORD has spoken against you) — the preposition 'al (against/upon) signals a covenant-lawsuit or judgment speech rather than a word of blessing. 'Al kol-hamishpachah (against the whole family) — the covenant family (mishpachah) is accountable as a unit; the exodus defines the boundaries of responsibility.
3.2: You alone have I known — raq etkhem yadati mikol mishpachot ha'adamah: the pivotal verse of the chapter and the theological heart of the book. Yada' (know) in its covenant sense — relational election, not merely cognitive awareness (cf. Gen 18:19; Jer 1:5). The raq (only, alone) is emphatic: Israel's election was exclusive among all families of earth. 'Al-ken ephqod 'aleykhem et kol-avonoteykhem (therefore I will hold you accountable for all your iniquities) — election intensifies accountability, not reduces it. The privilege of covenant relationship creates heightened obligation, not immunity.
3.3: Can two people walk together — hayo-lekhun shne'im yachdav bilti im-noa'du: the series of rhetorical questions (vv3-6) is Amos' argumentative method — from self-evident natural observations to the conclusion that YHWH's word creates prophetic compulsion. Noa'du (unless they have made an appointment/agreed) — the verb ya'ad (to set a time, appoint, agree to meet); the metaphor is two people with agreed direction. The question proves: effects have causes; YHWH's word has produced the prophet.
3.4: Does a lion roar when it has no prey — heyish'ag arye ba-ya'ar ve-tereph eyn lo: lion behavior as analogy for prophetic compulsion. The roar announces the kill; silence means no prey. Yitten kefir qolo mimme'onato bilti im-lakhad (does the young lion growl from its lair without having caught anything?) — kefir (young lion) vs. arye (mature lion) — the pair covers both stages. The roar of judgment (3:8 conclusion) is grounded in the reality of the prey already taken.
3.5: Does a bird fall to the ground without a trap — hettippol tsippor 'al-pach ha'arets: trap-and-bird imagery continuing the cause-effect series. Ve-moqesh eyn lah (when no trap has been set) — moqesh (snare, trap) is also used metaphorically for seductions/idols. Ha-ya'aleh-pach min-ha'adamah velakod lo yilkod (does a trap spring from the ground without catching anything?) — the trap springs because something triggered it; nothing happens without cause.
3.6: When a ram's horn sounds in a city — im-yitqa' shofar be'ir: the shofar alarm (military attack warning) produces fear by reflex. Ve-'am lo yecherod (do the people not tremble/fear?) — the expected answer: yes, they do. The final question is the theological climax: im-tihyeh ra'ah be'ir va-YHWH lo 'asah (if disaster strikes a city, has the LORD not done it?) — ra'ah (disaster, evil, calamity) — not moral evil but calamity/judgment. YHWH's sovereign governance of history: even disaster has divine agency behind it (cf. Isa 45:7).
3.7: The Lord GOD does nothing without disclosing his plans — ki lo ya'aseh Adonai YHWH dabar ki im-galah sodo el-'avadav haNevi'im: the climactic principle of the riddle-series. Sod (secret counsel, council) — the divine council concept (Jer 23:18, 22; Ps 25:14); YHWH's sod is his deliberative council-decision, first disclosed to the prophets before execution. The prophets are admitted to the divine inner circle (sod) and tasked with announcing what they heard. This verse legitimates the entire prophetic enterprise: Amos speaks because YHWH disclosed.
3.8: The lion has roared — who will not be afraid? — arye sha'ag mi lo yira': the answer to all the rhetorical questions of vv3-7 is stated: divine speech produces prophetic compulsion. Adonai YHWH dibber mi lo yinnabe' (the Lord GOD has spoken — who can withhold prophecy?) — nibba' (to prophesy, speak as a prophet) — the Niphal/Hithpael of naba'; prophetic speech is the natural response to divine speech, as fear is the natural response to the lion's roar. Amos' defense of his prophetic calling is built on this principle.
3.9: Proclaim to the citadels of Ashdod and Egypt — Amos summons foreign powers as witnesses to Israel's shame. Asafim al-hare Shomron (gather on the mountains of Samaria) — the witnesses are to observe from the heights. Mehumot rabbot (great disorders/tumults) and ashuqim (oppressions) — mehumah (chaos, confusion) is the term for covenant-curse disorder (Deut 28:20); 'oshek (oppression of the vulnerable) is the corresponding term for economic injustice. Even Philistine Ashdod and Egypt would be shocked by what they see.
3.10: They do not know how to act with integrity — lo-yad'u 'asot-nekhochah: nekhochah (what is right, straight, correct) — the term for straight, upright dealing; they do not even know what integrity is. Ne'um-YHWH (declares the LORD) — the prophetic utterance formula; the accusation carries divine authority. Ha'otzrim chamas va-shod be-armnoteihem (those who hoard violence and plunder in their fortresses) — chamas (violence, unjust gain) and shod (plunder, devastation) stored in the very fortresses built with stolen wealth.
3.11: An enemy will surround the land — tsar usseviv ha'arets: the judgment mirrors the crime. They hoarded plunder in their fortresses; those same fortresses will be plundered. Ve-horid mimekh uzzakh (he will strip away your strength/defense from you) — uzzah (strength, might) stripped away; the strength used to oppress becomes the first casualty. Ve-nivazzu armonotaykh (your fortresses will be plundered) — the ironic reversal: the storage site of shod (plunder) becomes the object of plundering.
3.12: As the shepherd snatches two legs or a scrap of an ear — ka'asher yatsel haro'eh mippi ha'arye shtey kraa'im o vadal-ozen: a vivid analogy. Mosaic law required shepherds to produce the remains of sheep killed by predators to prove they had not stolen the animal (Exod 22:13). The two legs and ear-scrap are not rescue but proof of death — the animal is entirely gone. Ken yinnatselv bene Yisra'el (so will the Israelites be rescued) — the irony: this is what passes for 'rescue'; survival means a tattered remnant, not genuine deliverance. Yosevei Shomron bipheat mittah uviqdat eres — the elite of Samaria, reclining on their expensive imported furniture, will be what the shepherd shows as 'evidence.'
3.13: Hear and testify against the house of Jacob — shim'u ve-ha'idu bebet Ya'aqov: the court language continues. Ne'um-Adonai YHWH Elohei Tzeva'ot — the full divine title: YHWH Elohei Tzeva'ot (LORD God of armies/hosts) appears first here in Amos; the military title frames the judgment that follows. The summons is to testify, not just to hear.
3.14: On the day I call Israel to account — beyom paqdi pish'ei Yisra'el 'alav: paqad (visit, reckon, hold accountable) — the term for divine intervention in judgment or blessing. Ve-paqad'ti 'al mizbechot Bet-El (I will strike down the altars of Bethel) — the apostate shrine of Bethel is targeted specifically; where Israel sought YHWH through the golden calf will be demolished. Ve-niggede'u qarnot hamizbeach (the altar's horns will be cut off and fall to the ground) — the horns of the altar were where sacrificial blood was applied and where a person could flee for asylum (1 Kgs 1:50-51; 2:28); both sanctuary and sacrifice are terminated.
3.15: The winter house and the summer house — beit ha-choref (winter house) and beit ha-qayits (summer house) — the royal and wealthy elite maintained seasonal residences; the existence of both signals extreme luxury. Batei hashschen (houses adorned with ivory) — sheshen (ivory/tooth) used as furniture inlay and wall decoration; this is confirmed archaeologically at Samaria where ivory plaques were found. Ve-safu batim rabbim (and the great mansions will come to an end) — suf (come to an end, be swept away); the accumulation of property that secured status will be swept away in a single judgment.

4.1: Cows of Bashan — parot ha-Bashan: the address to wealthy Samaritan women as cattle is sharply satirical. Bashan was celebrated for its fat, fertile cattle (Deut 32:14; Ps 22:12; Ezek 39:18). The image is of well-fed, complacent animals making demands: ha-omrot la'adonehem havi'u ve-nishteh (saying to their husbands, 'Bring us something to drink!') — the drinking-demand is the surface expression of an entire lifestyle built on the exploitation of the poor and needy (ha'osheqot dallim, harortsetsot evyonim).
4.2: The Lord GOD has sworn by his holiness — nishba' Adonai YHWH bequodsho: the divine oath formula with bequodsho (by his holiness) — the most absolute form of divine oath; YHWH swears by his own nature, which cannot be compromised. Ki hinneh yamim ba'im 'aleykhem (days are coming upon you) — the prophetic future; ve-nissa' etkhem be-tsinnot (they will drag you away with hooks/thorns) — tsinnah (thorn, hook; possibly fishing hook) — the image of fishhook deportation (cf. Ezek 29:4; 38:4 for the fishhook imagery).
4.3: Through the breaches in the walls — u-ferutsot tetse'na — they will go out through the breaches (perets) in the city walls made by the enemy assault, ishah neggdah (each woman straight before her), marched out in single file in the captive procession. Ve-hishlakhten ha-Harmonah — 'flung toward Harmon' — Harmonah is uncertain; possibly a proper place name for an exile destination or a textual crux. The image of exile is certain; the specific destination is not.
4.4: Come to Bethel and rebel — bo'u Vet-El u-fish'u ha-Gilgal harbu lifshoa': the biting sarcasm of this liturgical parody. Amos uses the form of a priestly call to worship (cf. Ps 95:1-2; 100:4) but replaces the sacred invitation with the accusation: pasha' (rebel, transgress) replaces worship. Bethel was the royal shrine of the north; Gilgal was an ancient cultic site near Jericho associated with covenant renewal (Josh 4-5; 1 Sam 11:14-15). Havi'u laboqer zivcheykhem (bring your sacrifices every morning) — the frequency of sacrifice is turned from piety to evidence of rebellion.
4.5: Offer your leavened thanksgiving sacrifices — ve-qatter mechamets todah: leavened offerings were prohibited from the altar (Lev 2:11) — either Amos is describing an actual illegal practice or using the leaven-prohibition symbolically for the corrupted thanksgiving offering. Ve-qir'u nedavot hash'mi'u (proclaim your freewill offerings, announce them publicly) — nedavah (freewill offering) is by definition supposed to be private and voluntary; publicizing it transforms it into performance of piety for social prestige. Ki ken ahavtem bene Yisra'el (for this is what you love to do) — the indictment: the motive is love of religious display, not love of God.
4.6: I gave you empty mouths — ve-gam ani natati lakhem niqyon shinayim: niqyon shinayim (cleanness/emptiness of teeth) — a vivid idiomatic expression for famine; nothing to eat = teeth that never get dirty. This begins the five-fold refrain: ve-lo shavtem elai ne'um-YHWH (yet you did not return to me, declares the LORD). Each judgment in the series (famine, drought, blight, plague, overthrow) was designed as a covenant-discipline calling for teshuvah (return/repentance), but none produced the response.
4.7: I also withheld the rain — ve-gam anochi manati mikkem et-hageshem: the covenant-curse of drought (Deut 28:23-24; Lev 26:19). The drought is spatially selective: ha'ir achat umtartem ve-ha'ir achat lo amtir (I sent rain on one city but not another) — demonstrating that the meteorological pattern was not natural but divinely governed. Chalqa achat yimmatertter ve-chalqa asher lo tammtir 'aleiha tivasesh (one piece of ground was rained on while another dried up) — even within the same district: YHWH controls the rain to a parcel-by-parcel level.
4.8: Two or three cities staggered — ve-na'u shtayim shalosh 'arim el-'ir achat: nu' (stagger/wander) captures physical weakness from thirst; they do not march to a city but stagger. Ve-lo yisbe'u (and could not get enough) — the water they found was insufficient. The famine and drought discipline aimed at shuv (return); it was not punitive destruction but corrective summons. Yet the refrain: they did not return.
4.9: I struck your crops with blight and mildew — hikiti etkhem ba-shiddafon uve-yerakon: shiddafon (blight, east wind damage; probably the hot sirocco that scorches grain) and yerakon (mildew, yellowing disease). Harbut gannotechem uve-kharmechem (your many gardens and vineyards) — the accumulation of crops signals prosperous agriculture, all of which the locusts (hazegav) consumed. The refrain: they did not return.
4.10: I sent among you a plague like those of Egypt — shilachti va-khem dever be-derekh Mitsrayim: the exodus-plague is the model for the covenant-curse judgment. Haragti va-cherev bacheireikhem (I killed your young men with the sword) — military defeat framed as divine killing. Bashvi suseikhem (took your horses captive) — the military establishment (horses, chariots) is captured. Va-a'aleh beosh machanekhem (I made the stench of your camps rise) — the stench of unburied dead, the ultimate defilement and disgrace. Refrain: they did not return.
4.11: I overthrew some of you as God overthrew Sodom and Gomorrah — haphakhti vakhem ka'asher hafakh Elohim et-Sedom ve-et-Amorah: the Sodom-Gomorrah overthrow (hafakh = overturn, demolish) is the paradigm of total annihilation. Va-tehi khe'ud mutssal mi-sreifah (you were like a stick snatched from the fire) — the ud (burning stick, firebrand) rescued from fire — nearly destroyed; the metaphor recurs at Zech 3:2 for Joshua the high priest's near-destruction. Refrain: they did not return.
4.12: Prepare yourself to meet your God, Israel — lachen koh e'eseh lekha Yisra'el (therefore, because I will do this to you): the five-fold discipline has failed. The confrontation is now direct. Hikkon liqra'at Elohekha Yisra'el: hikkon (prepare yourself, be ready) — the Niphal imperative of kun (to be established, firm, ready); the same root is used for preparation before theophany (Exod 19:11, 15). Qara'at (to meet, toward meeting) — the encounter is forced, not invited. Israel will meet YHWH not as worshiper but as defendant.
4.13: For this is he who forms the mountains and creates the wind — the first of three doxological hymn-fragments in Amos (4:13; 5:8-9; 9:5-6). Yotser harim u-vore ruach (who forms the mountains and creates the wind) — the same two verbs of creation as in Gen 1-2: yatsar (form/fashion, as a potter) and bara' (create, the divine creative act used exclusively of God's activity). U-maggid le-adam mah-seho (who reveals to humanity what he is thinking) — seho = his thought/concern; the creator God who made the mountains also communicates with his creatures through prophets. YHWH Elohei Tzeva'ot shemo (the LORD God of armies is his name) — the doxology closes with the divine war-title; the creator is also the commander of all forces.

5.1: The funeral song I take up over you — qinah asher ani nose' 'aleykhem: qinah is the Hebrew funeral lament, characterized by a distinctive 3+2 beat meter (qinah meter) — three stressed syllables followed by two, mimicking the stumbling gait of grief. Amos is singing Israel's funeral before the death occurs — the prophetic perfect expressing certainty of coming events as already accomplished. The form is as striking as the content: the living are addressed in their own lament-song.
5.2: She has fallen, never to rise again — nafela lo-tosif qum betulat Yisra'el: betulah (virgin/maiden) as a personification of the nation emphasizes both youth and vulnerability; a young woman who should have a future. Natsha 'al-admatah eyn meqimah (abandoned on her own land, with no one to lift her up) — natash (forsaken, abandoned) + ein meqimah (no one to raise her) — two absences: no rescuer, no restoration. The qinah meter can be felt in the Hebrew rhythm.
5.3: The city that sent out a thousand will have a hundred survivors — ha'ir hayotse'et elef tashir me'ah: a devastating attrition ratio: 90% losses. A city that fielded a thousand soldiers will return a hundred; a hundred will return ten. Le-vet Yisra'el (to the house of Israel) — the remnant calculation is made in relationship to Israel's national structure. The mathematical precision is rhetorical: the losses are absolute and calculable.
5.4: Seek me and live — dorshuni vi-cheyu: the pivot from doom to invitation. Darash (seek, inquire, resort to) is the technical term for seeking an oracle at a sanctuary (2 Kgs 1:2; 22:18) but also for genuine turning to YHWH. The command is brief and total: seek me, not the shrine. Vi-cheyu (and live) — the covenant life-or-death formula (Deut 30:19-20): seeking YHWH produces life; the alternative is death.
5.5: Do not seek Bethel; do not go to Gilgal — the three apostate pilgrimage sites: Vet-El (Bethel), ha-Gilgal (Gilgal), Be'er-Sheva (Beersheba). Bethel and Dan had golden calves set up by Jeroboam I (1 Kgs 12:29); Gilgal had degenerated from its covenant-renewal history; Beersheba in Judah had also become a pilgrimage site. Ki ha-Gilgal galoh yigle (for Gilgal will certainly go into exile) — wordplay: Gilgal-galoh-yigle (the place-name, the infinitive absolute, the imperfect) creates a triple sound-resonance. Ve-Vet-El yihyeh le-aven (Bethel will be reduced to nothing/nothingness) — aven (nothingness/wickedness) is a pun on Bethel (house of God) becoming Beth-Aven (house of nothing); cf. Hosea 4:15; 10:5.
5.6: Seek the LORD and live — dirshu et-YHWH vi-cheyu: the imperative repeated with YHWH as the direct object, making explicit what v4 implied. Pen yitslach ke-esh vet Yosef (or he will sweep through the house of Joseph like a fire) — Joseph = the northern kingdom (Ephraim/Manasseh). The fire image from the oracle-series returns; now YHWH himself is the consuming fire if seeking is refused. Ve-akhlah ve-eyn mekhabbeh le-Vet-El (devouring it with no one to put it out at Bethel) — the shrine that was supposed to be the fire of sacred offering becomes the target of consuming judgment.
5.7: You who twist justice into bitter poison — ha-hofkhim la-la'anah mishpat: la'anah (wormwood, absinth) — a bitter plant (Deut 29:17; Jer 9:14; Rev 8:11) used as a metaphor for the bitterness of perverted justice. Ve-tsedaqah la-arets hinihu (and hurl righteousness to the ground) — hinniach (cause to rest, lay down) used here with violent force: righteousness is thrown down. The two covenant virtues — mishpat (justice) and tsedaqah (righteousness) — the same pair that appears in 5:24 — are here both destroyed.
5.8: He who made the Pleiades and Orion — 'oseh Kimah ve-Kesil: the second doxological hymn-fragment (5:8-9). Kimah (Pleiades — the seven-star cluster) and Kesil (Orion — the giant constellation) are named in Job 9:9; 38:31-32 as examples of divine creative power. Ve-hafokheth la-boqer tsalmaveth (who turns deep darkness into dawn) — tsalmaveth (shadow of death, deep darkness) reversed to boqer (morning/dawn): the cosmic reversal of darkness to light is YHWH's routine act. Yom layla hechshikh (and darkens the day into night) — the same power works in both directions. Ha-qore' le-me ha-yam (who summons the waters of the sea) — the flood/deluge theme; YHWH controls the waters that could overwhelm the earth.
5.9: He sends destruction flashing against the powerful — ha-mavlig shod al-az: balag (make flash/gleam; some read 'smile upon' or 'bring suddenly') — the connotation of lightning-speed destruction overtaking the strong. Ve-shod al-mibtsar yavo' (so that ruin comes upon the fortified city) — the armonot (fortresses) of the accusation are the target of the creator's power. The doxology grounds the judgment in creation-sovereignty: the God who made the stars and controls seas and darkness can certainly destroy human fortresses.
5.10: They hate the judge who rebukes them at the gate — sane'u va-sha'ar mokhiach: sha'ar (city gate) was the location of legal proceedings, commercial transactions, and civic life; the elder-judges who sat at the gate (shoftim ba-sha'ar) were the embodiment of public justice. Mokhiach (one who rebukes, corrects, adjudicates) — the proper judicial function hated. Ve-dover tamim yete'avuhu (and they despise the one who tells the truth/speaks with integrity) — tamim (blameless, complete, truthful) — honest witness was anathema to those who controlled justice by bribes.
5.11: You crush the poor and impose a grain tax — ya'an boshaskhem al-dal: boshaskhem from bas or bush — the precise sense is debated; 'trample, crush, press' captures it. U-mas bar tiqqechu mimmennu (you impose a grain tax on him) — mas bar (grain levy/rental) — demanding grain-rent from tenant farmers. The ironic reversal follows: batim gazit beniitem ve-lo teshvu bam (you have built houses of dressed stone but will not live in them) — gazit (hewn/dressed stone) indicates luxury construction; the covenant-curse of Deut 28:30 ('you will build a house but not live in it') is explicitly echoed.
5.12: I know how numerous your crimes are and how great your sins — ki yadati rabbim pish'eykhem: YHWH's direct first-person statement with yada' (know) — the covenant-knowing that began with Israel's election (3:2) now functions in judgment. Three charges in one verse: tsarrei tsaddiq (oppressors of the innocent/righteous), loquchei kofer (those who accept bribes — kofer = ransom, payment to avoid justice), and evyonim ba-sha'ar hittum (who deny justice to the needy at the gate) — the gate-justice system completely corrupted.
5.13: Therefore the wise man stays silent at a time like this — lachen hamaskil ba'et hahi yiddom: maskil (discerning person, wise one) — one who understands the situation. Yiddom (stays silent) — the root damam (be still, silent) used for stunned grief and prudential silence when speech is futile or dangerous. Ki et ra'ah hi (for it is an evil time) — the situation is so corrupted that even the wise person's counsel cannot be heard; speaking into a deaf system is futile. This verse anticipates the kind of contextual prudence taught in Ecclesiastes.
5.14: Seek good and not evil, that you may live — dirshu tov ve-al-ra' lema'an tich'yu: the seek-and-live formula from vv4-6 now applied to moral categories rather than cultic ones: seek tov (good) rather than YHWH vs. the apostate shrines. The two formulations together make the point: seeking YHWH and seeking good are inseparable. Vi-yehi khen YHWH Elohei Tzeva'ot ittekhem (and then the LORD, the God of armies, will be with you) — the divine-with-you presence (immanuel) is the result of seeking the good; the conditional is striking: divine presence is connected to ethical seeking.
5.15: Hate evil and love good; establish justice at the gate — sinu ra' ve-'ahavu tov: the emotional verbs are as strong as possible — not merely choose or prefer but sane' (hate) evil and 'ahav (love) good. Ve-hatstsegu va-sha'ar mishpat (establish justice at the gate) — the gate-justice system, which was corrupted in v10, must be reformed. The result is tentative: ulai yechannen YHWH Elohei Tzeva'ot she'erit Yosef (perhaps the LORD… will be gracious to the remnant of Joseph) — the ulai (perhaps) of divine sovereign freedom; even repentance does not compel YHWH. The remnant (she'erit) concept appears: there may be survivors, but it is YHWH's prerogative.
5.16: In all the public squares there will be wailing — be-khol-rechavot misped: rechovot (public squares, broad streets) and kol-chutsot (all the streets) — the mourning fills every public space. Qar'u ikkar el-eivel (farmers will be summoned to mourn) — ikkar (farmer, plowman) is called from agricultural work to the funeral; farming stops. Ve-eliqinah el-misped (and professional mourners to lead the lament) — yode'ei nehi (those skilled in lamentation) — the professional mourning-guilds whose wailing was hired for funerals; Israel is so comprehensively dead that even the professional mourners are insufficient.
5.17: Wailing in every vineyard — uve-khol-keramim misped: the vineyard (place of joy and harvest celebration) becomes a place of mourning. Ki-e'evor beqirbeka (for I will pass through your midst, declares the LORD) — the Passover vocabulary is unmistakable: 'avar (pass through) is the verb for YHWH's act in Exod 12:12 ('I will pass through the land of Egypt'). The first Passover was death passing over Israel while striking Egypt; now YHWH passes through Israel bringing death. The protection of Passover is reversed for a faithless covenant people.
5.18: Woe to you who long for the day of the LORD — hoy ha-mit'avvim et yom YHWH: the first explicit treatment of yom YHWH (the Day of the LORD) in the writing prophets. The concept predates Amos (the people 'long for' it as an established expectation), but Amos inverts its popular meaning. Israel expected the Day to bring military victory for Israel against her enemies — a day of divine intervention on their behalf. Amos announces: lamah-zeh lakhem yom YHWH hu-choshek ve-lo-or (Why would you want the day of the LORD? It is darkness, not light) — the inversion is total. Those confident in covenant status will find judgment, not deliverance.
5.19: Like a man who fled from a lion and ran into a bear — ka'asher yanus ish mipnei ha-arye u-paga'o ha-dov: a vivid chain of escalating inescapability. Lion → bear → serpent: each escape from one danger leads directly into the next. U-va ha-vayit ve-samakh yado al-haqqir u-neshako ha-nachash (escaped into his house and put his hand on the wall only to be bitten by a snake) — even the domestic refuge is lethal. The three-threat chain illustrates that the Day of the LORD cannot be outrun or outmaneuvered by any human strategy.
5.20: Is not the day of the LORD darkness and not light — halo-choshek yom YHWH ve-lo-or: the rhetorical question demands agreement; the expected answer is 'yes, of course.' Ve-afelah ve-lo-naggah lo (pitch dark, with no gleam of brightness) — two synonyms for darkness: afelah (thick darkness) and the negation of naggah (gleam/brightness). The total elimination of light-expectation seals the inversion begun in v18.
5.21: I hate and despise your religious festivals — sane'ti me'asti chaggeykhem: the divine first-person rejection of the cult is stated with maximum emotional force: sane' (hate) + ma'as (despise, reject utterly). Chaggim (religious festivals, pilgrimage feasts) — the three prescribed pilgrimage feasts of Israel (Exod 23:14-17; Lev 23). Ve-lo ariach be-'atsaroteykhem (I take no pleasure in your solemn gatherings) — 'atsarah (sacred assembly, solemn gathering) — the formal convocations are rejected. The rejection is of the form without substance: not of festival observance per se but of festival observance that coexists with injustice.
5.22: Even when you bring me burnt offerings and grain offerings — ki im-ta'alu li olot u-minchoteykhem: the three major offering categories: 'olah (burnt offering — completely burned), minchah (grain offering), and shlamim (fellowship/peace offerings — shared meal). All are rejected. Lo ertsah (I will not accept them) and lo abbit (I will not look at them) — two formulas of divine rejection: neither the offerings nor the one offering them receives divine attention. The cult has become a substitute for covenant relationship rather than its expression.
5.23: Away with the noise of your songs — hasir meiali hamon shireykhah: hamon (noise, tumult) applied to the songs — what should be worship has become loud performance. Ve-zimrat nevalekha lo eshma' (I will not listen to the music of your harps) — nevel (harp, lute) was the prestige instrument of Levitical worship; its rejection is the rejection of the entire musical apparatus of the cult. The relationship between the three rejected elements (vv21-23) moves inward: festivals → sacrifices → music, covering the entire ritual system.
5.24: But let justice roll down like waters, and righteousness like an ever-flowing stream — ve-yiggal ka-mayim mishpat u-tsedaqah ke-nachal eitan: the counterpoint to vv21-23. Gal (roll, surge) — an active rolling force, not a trickle. Nachal eitan (ever-flowing stream) — eitan (perennial, constant, permanent) contrasts with the wadi that flows only seasonally; the justice YHWH demands must be permanent, not occasional. The pair mishpat (justice — procedural, judicial) and tsedaqah (righteousness — relational, covenant-faithful) is the Amos signature-phrase (also 5:7; 6:12); together they describe the full texture of covenant life that Israel has abandoned.
5.25: Did you bring me sacrifices during the forty years in the wilderness? — ha-zevachim u-minchah higgashtem li va-midbar arba'im shanah: the question is rhetorical with an implied answer of 'no' — the wilderness generation is idealized as a period before the corrupted cult of Canaan took root (cf. Jer 7:21-23; Hos 9:10). The point is not a literal denial of wilderness sacrifice (the Pentateuch records sacrifices) but a prophetic idealization: the covenant relationship with YHWH was primary; sacrifice was supposed to serve it, not replace it.
5.26: You carried your shrine of Sikkuth and Kiyyun — u-nesa'atem et Sikkut malkeykhem ve-et Kiyyun tsalmeykhom: Sikkuth and Kiyyun (or Chiun) are star-deities — probably Assyrian Sakkut and Kaiwanu (the planet Saturn). The MT vocalizes with the vowels of sheqets (detestable thing) to indicate contempt. Kochav eloheykhem (your star-god) — astral worship combined with YHWH worship. Stephen's speech in Acts 7:42-43 cites the LXX version of this verse, which names Rephan (for Kiyyun) and applies it to Israel's idolatry in the wilderness.
5.27: Therefore I will send you into exile beyond Damascus — ve-higleti etkhem me-halah le-Dammasheq: the judgment clause at the end of the oracle sequence. Exile beyond Damascus points northeast — into Assyria, which lies beyond Damascus. The deportation of the northern kingdom by Assyria (722 BC) fulfilled this directly (2 Kgs 17:6). Amar YHWH Elohei Tzeva'ot shemo (says the LORD, whose name is the God of armies) — the closing divine title grounds the judgment oracle in the one who controls armies and history.
